result: I keep thinking about the small quiet that lives between two heartbeats—how it’s the only real estate left where nothing is for sale. No ads pop up there, no algorithm can predict its shape. I go there when the news gets too loud, when my phone forgets it’s a tool and acts like a leash. Last Tuesday I pressed my tongue to the roof of my mouth just to remember I have a body that isn’t data. The taste was metallic, like the keys on my grandfather’s typewriter, the one he used to write love letters in 1953. He never used backspace; he said mistakes are just plot twists. I think about that when I delete half a text seventeen times. Somewhere in the cloud there’s a graveyard of unsent apologies floating like ghosts with bad Wi-Fi. Meanwhile the moon keeps rising without requiring a subscription, and ants keep building condos beneath the sidewalk, oblivious to crypto. I watched a beetle roll a ball of dung three times its size uphill for twenty minutes, and it felt more honest than any TED Talk. We keep trying to outrun death with productivity hacks, but decay is just another word for collaboration: my skin practicing to be soil, my breath learning to be tree. Tomorrow I’m going to fail at something on purpose—maybe burn toast, maybe forget a name—just to keep the perfectionists nervous. The world is too beautiful to be useful, too broken to be sold. I want to be a crack in the vase that lets the light leak out, evidence that emptiness also has a shape.

No ads pop up there, no algorithm can predict its shape. I go there when the news gets too loud, when my phone forgets it’s a tool and acts like a leash. The taste was metallic, like the keys on my grandfather’s typewriter, the one he used to write love letters in 1953. He never used backspace; he said mistakes are just plot twists. I think about that when I delete half a text seventeen times. Somewhere in the cloud there’s a graveyard of unsent apologies floating like ghosts with bad Wi-Fi. Meanwhile the moon keeps rising without requiring a subscription, and ants keep building condos beneath the sidewalk, oblivious to crypto. I watched a beetle roll a ball of dung three times its size uphill for twenty minutes, and it felt more honest than any TED Talk. We keep trying to outrun death with productivity hacks, but decay is just another word for collaboration: my skin practicing to be soil, my breath learning to be tree. Tomorrow I’m going to fail at something on purpose—maybe burn toast, maybe forget a name—just to keep the perfectionists nervous. The world is too beautiful to be useful, too broken to be sold. I want to be a crack in the vase that lets the light leak out, evidence that emptiness also has a shape.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
